did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hyper-parameter optimized Fully Convolution Encoder Decoder Network (FCEDN), a deep learning model, for skin cancer image segmentation. The hyper-parameters are optimized using a novel Exponential Neighborhood Grey Wolf Optimization (EN-GWO) algorithm. The study validates the model on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2017 dermoscopy image datasets, achieving specific segmentation metrics (Jaccard, Dice, accuracy) and comparing performance against other deep learning models like U-Net, Link-Net, SegNet, and FCN.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel hyper-parameter optimized Fully Convolution Encoder Decoder Network (FCEDN) using an Exponential Neighborhood Grey Wolf Optimization (EN-GWO) algorithm. It includes a methodology section detailing the model architecture and optimization process, experimental results on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2017 datasets with specific performance metrics (Jaccard, Dice, accuracy), and comparisons against other deep learning models (U-Net, Link-Net, SegNet, FCN). The abstract explicitly states 'in this paper, a hyper-parameter optimized FCEDN is proposed' and reports new validation results, distinguishing it from a review paper.

title: Skin cancer image segmentation utilizing a novel EN-GWO based hyper-parameter optimized FCEDN
journal: Journal of King Saud University – Computer and Information Sciences
brief_summary: This study proposes a hyper-parameter optimized Fully Convolution Encoder Decoder Network (FCEDN) for automated skin cancer image segmentation in dermoscopy images. The network's hyper-parameters are optimized using a novel Exponential Neighborhood Grey Wolf Optimization (EN-GWO) algorithm to balance exploration and exploitation. The model was validated on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2017 datasets, achieving Jaccard coefficients of 96.41% and 86.85%, Dice coefficients of 98.48% and 87.23%, and accuracies of 98.32% and 95.25%, respectively. The proposed EN-GWO optimized FCEDN outperformed other deep learning models such as U-Net, Link-Net, SegNet, and FCN, as well as other optimization techniques like GA and PSO. The results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed model in accurately segmenting skin cancer regions and identifying lesion boundaries.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on the segmentation of skin cancer lesions in dermoscopy images using the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2017 datasets. While melanoma is mentioned in the introduction as a hazardous form of skin cancer, the study treats the target as a general 'skin cancer' class for segmentation purposes without distinguishing specific histological subtypes in the experimental results.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer lesion masks in dermoscopy images
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a Fully Convolution Encoder Decoder Network (FCEDN) for pixel-level semantic segmentation of skin cancer regions. The model predicts binary masks separating the lesion from the background. Performance is evaluated using Jaccard coefficient, Dice coefficient, and accuracy on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2017 datasets.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic skin cancer image dataset collected from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) for the year 2016.

use.training: Used for training the hyper-parameter optimized FCEDN model (70% split).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; validation metrics are derived from the testing set or internal cross-validation not detailed in counts.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluating segmentation performance (Jaccard, Dice, Accuracy) and comparing against other models (30% split).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic skin cancer image dataset collected from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) for the year 2017.

use.training: Used for training the hyper-parameter optimized FCEDN model (70% split).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; validation metrics are derived from the testing set or internal cross-validation not detailed in counts.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluating segmentation performance (Jaccard, Dice, Accuracy) and comparing against other models (30% split).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the efficacy of the EN-GWO optimization algorithm against other nature-inspired techniques (GWO, iGWO, eGWO, mGWO, GA, PSO) on benchmark functions, reporting mean and standard deviation of fitness error.
To summarize the performance results (fitness error) of optimization algorithms over benchmark functions across 50 independent runs.

To evaluate and compare the segmentation performance of the proposed EN-GWO optimized FCEDN model against other deep learning models (U-Net, Link-Net, SegNet, FCN) and optimization techniques on ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2017 datasets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests, ANOVA) for the comparison of segmentation metrics. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation over 50 runs) and direct metric values (Jaccard, Dice, Accuracy).

ISIC 2016.notes: The proposed EN-GWO based hyper-parameter optimized FCEDN model outperformed other recent deep learning models such as U-Net, Link-Net, SegNet, and FCN. It produced an improvement of 12.37%, 12.76%, 16.86% and 14.23% in Jaccard coefficient and 7.28%, 7.16%, 11.4% and 9.54% in dice coefficient compared to CNN (Xie et al., 2020); DPFCN (Nasr-Esfahani et al., 2019); FCN-8s (Long et al., 2015) and U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) respectively.

ISIC 2017.notes: The proposed model results in an increment of 28.47%; 19.79%, 21.81%, 23.54%, 10.91%, 13.76%, 40.99%, 24.42%, 17.36%,18.16%,24.78% and 24.25% in Jaccard coefficient and 8.09%, 3.72%, 4.72%, 5.61%, 1.19%, 3.16%, 14.33%, 9.31%, 5.22%, 5.73%, 6.25%, and 9.86% in dice coefficient compared to the FCN-AlexNet (Kaymak et al., 2020); FCN-8s (Kaymak et al., 2020); FCN-16s (Kaymak et al., 2020); FCN-32s (Kaymak et al., 2020); CNN (Xie et al., 2020); FC-DPN (Shan et al., 2020); SegNet (Al-masni et al., 2018); U-Net16 (Tschandl et al., 2019); LinkNet34 (Tschandl et al., 2019); LinkNet152 (Tschandl et al., 2019); U-Net (Goyal et al., 2019); and II-FCN (Wen, 2017) respectively. Note: There is a discrepancy in the reported Jaccard coefficient for the proposed model on ISIC 2017 between the abstract (86.85%) and Table 7 (86.88%).

conclusions: The proposed hyper-parameter optimized Fully Convolution Encoder Decoder Network (FCEDN) using the Exponential Neighborhood Grey Wolf Optimization (EN-GWO) algorithm effectively automates skin cancer image segmentation in dermoscopy images.

The EN-GWO algorithm provides a better balance between exploration and exploitation compared to other optimization techniques (GWO, iGWO, eGWO, mGWO, GA, PSO), resulting in superior hyper-parameter optimization for the FCEDN model.

The proposed EN-GWO based FCEDN model outperformed other recent deep learning models such as U-Net, Link-Net, SegNet, and FCN, as well as other optimization-based FCEDN variants, in terms of segmentation accuracy.
On the ISIC 2016 dataset, the proposed model achieved a Jaccard coefficient of 96.41%, Dice coefficient of 98.48%, and accuracy of 98.32%.

On the ISIC 2017 dataset, the proposed model achieved a Jaccard coefficient of 86.85% (reported as 86.88% in detailed results), Dice coefficient of 87.23% (reported as 86.99% in detailed results), and accuracy of 95.25% (reported as 92.29% in detailed results).
Manual fine-tuning of hyper-parameters for FCEDN is time-consuming and error-prone, whereas the automated EN-GWO optimization significantly improves segmentation performance.

Input image size impacts performance; while smaller sizes (e.g., 50x50) reduce training time, they drastically reduce feature extraction capability, whereas 100x100 pixels yielded a higher Jaccard coefficient in preliminary tests.
The model demonstrates potential for application in other classification and image segmentation tasks beyond skin cancer.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; performance was evaluated solely on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2017 datasets, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison of segmentation metrics against baseline models.

There is a discrepancy in reported results for the ISIC 2017 dataset: the abstract reports a Jaccard coefficient of 86.85% and Dice coefficient of 87.23%, while Table 7 reports 86.88% and 86.99% respectively.
The model was trained on images resized to 100x100 pixels, which may lead to loss of fine-grained boundary details critical for precise lesion segmentation.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which may contribute to potential overfitting given the relatively small dataset sizes (900 images for ISIC 2016, 2000 for ISIC 2017).

The authors acknowledge that the datasets primarily contain single, centered lesions with apparent boundaries, excluding challenging cases like multiple lesions or sub-damaged skin, which limits the clinical applicability of the findings.
The study relies on comparative metrics fetched from original papers for baseline models rather than re-running them on the same data, which may introduce inconsistencies in evaluation conditions.
